interactions.summary: The host asks how Jiang would read a mirror-image foreign influencer teaching Chinese topics; Jiang says he would be baffled, then narrates his own strangeness: Yale, China, rejecting money, and manifesting his unlikely public role.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Interview exchange: SPEAKER_00 is host Jack Neel or ad read; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang unless noted.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization is consistent enough for this packet: SPEAKER_00 carries host questions, intro, outro, or sponsor reads; SPEAKER_01 carries Jiang answers. Sponsor ad segments are not Jiang claims.

claims.claim: Jiang says his career makes sense less as espionage than as a long pattern of strange choices: Yale to China, family bafflement, high-school teaching, rejecting study-abroad riches, and walking away from UN comfort.
Jiang says he manifested his public role by imagining the possibility, studying alternative media, and positioning himself for debate, conversation, and truth-seeking venues.

signature_moments.moment: The United Nations is described as royal comfort and meaningless existence.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I was treated, like royalty... And I walked away.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows his repeated refusal of conventional success.

signature_moments.moment: The YouTube strategy begins as making stupid predictions that either humiliate him forever or get him on Tucker Carlson.
signature_moments.source_phrase: make some stupid predictions and hope they turn out correct
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains the channel as deliberate risk-taking, not accidental virality.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: For Jiang, imagining a possible future and maneuvering himself into position for it.
Rogan, Dore, the Weinsteins, Tucker, and similar spaces Jiang saw as truth-seeking conversation venues.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates his alternative-media turn to the COVID period, about five years before the 2026 interview.
